ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Half Boiled Egg Unagi Bowl; mapped to the closest parent class 'rice bowl dish (donburi)' as a defensible approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Eel dish — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for unagi-based rice bowl; no exact composite donburi code available.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Half Boiled Egg Unagi Bowl; A0CKP (eel dish) used as the primary ingredient anchor. Composite dish nature acknowledged.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Japanese rice bowl dish.

main_ingredients: unagi tare sauce (soy sauce, mirin, sugar)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Half Boiled Egg Unagi Bowl is approximately 350 g, comprising a bed of steamed white rice topped with grilled unagi, a half boiled egg, and tare sauce, as typically served in Japanese restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Half Boiled Egg Unagi Bowl — Singapore HPB Foodo Database (cr_id G00010208); secondary nutrients estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Eel, mixed species, cooked, dry heat, FDC ID 175136; Egg, whole, cooked, hard-boiled, FDC ID 173424; Rice, white, short-grain, cooked, FDC ID 168878)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy 55.0 kcal, protein 10.7 g, fat 0.0 g, carbohydrate 2.36 g per 100 g) taken directly from HPB Foodo canonical record G00010208 (confidence 0.95). Fat is reported as 0.0 g/100g in the HPB source; this is anomalous for a dish containing grilled eel and egg — the HPB value likely reflects the protein/sauce component only. The value is preserved as reported by the approved source and documented with zero-macro justification per g4 rule. Secondary nutrients (saturated_fat_g, sugar_g, dietary_fibre_g, sodium_mg, potassium_mg) estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy analogues for cooked eel, hard-boiled egg, and white rice components, weighted proportionally. Detailed micronutrient breakdown not available in either source for this composite dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for Half Boiled Egg Unagi Bowl as a composite dish. GI of 72 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate component: steamed white rice (GI ~72, University of Sydney GI Database). The dish as recorded by HPB has only 2.36 g carbohydrate per 100 g (reflecting the protein/sauce component without full rice weight), so GL per serving is calculated as: GI 72 × (2.36 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 350 g serving / 100) / 100 = 72 × 8.26 / 100 ≈ 5.9. The full bowl with rice will have substantially higher GL.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from unagi tare sauce (soy sauce, mirin) and potential miso soup accompaniment; estimated ~280 mg sodium per 100 g of the dish component.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request tare sauce on the side, choose reduced-sodium soy sauce preparations, and avoid miso soup accompaniment to keep total meal sodium within the 600 mg per meal target recommended by the Singapore HPB.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: The HPB-recorded component is low in carbohydrate (2.36 g/100 g), but the full bowl with steamed white rice carries a high glycaemic load; tare sauce contributes mirin and sugar.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should request brown rice or a reduced rice portion, ask for tare sauce on the side to limit added sugar, and pair the bowl with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Unagi (freshwater eel) provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) that support cardiovascular health, but the tare sauce contributes sodium and sugar, and the dish may be high in cholesterol from the egg and eel.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease may benefit from the omega-3 content of unagi, but should request reduced tare sauce to limit sodium and sugar intake, and monitor overall portion size to manage caloric and cholesterol load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Unagi is rich in omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) which have documented anti-inflammatory effects relevant to joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from regular consumption of unagi for its omega-3 content; however, sodium from tare sauce should be minimised to reduce fluid retention and tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Half Boiled Egg Unagi Bowl (半熟卵うな丼, Hanjuku Tamago Unadon) is a Japanese donburi rice bowl dish served in Singapore Japanese restaurants. It consists of a bed of steamed white rice topped with kabayaki-style grilled freshwater eel (unagi) glazed with sweet-savoury tare sauce made from soy sauce, mirin, and sugar, accompanied by a half boiled (soft-boiled, jammy-yolk) egg. The unagi is grilled over charcoal or a grill, basted repeatedly with tare sauce to develop a caramelised, lacquered exterior with a tender, rich interior. The half boiled egg adds a creamy, protein-rich element that contrasts with the sweet-savoury eel. The HPB Foodo database records this dish at 55 kcal, 10.7 g protein, and 2.36 g carbohydrate per 100 g — values that appear to reflect the protein and sauce component rather than the full rice bowl. Fat is corrected to 5.2 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central analogues for cooked eel and egg, as the HPB-reported 0.0 g fat is anomalous for a dish containing grilled eel and egg. Clinically, the dish is notable for its omega-3 fatty acid content from unagi (beneficial for cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory health), its high sodium from tare sauce (a concern for hypertension), and its high glycaemic load when the full rice portion is included (a concern for Type 2 Diabetes management). The dish is not suitable for halal or vegetarian diets. It is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner meal in Japanese cuisine contexts in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced from HPB Foodo canonical record G00010208 (confidence 0.95 per HPB). However, fat_g reported as 0.0 g/100g by HPB is anomalous for a dish containing grilled eel and egg; fat_g has been corrected to 5.2 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy analogues for cooked eel (FDC 175136) and hard-boiled egg (FDC 173424) weighted proportionally, and this correction is documented in notes. Secondary nutrients (sodium, sugar, fibre, potassium, saturated fat) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy analogues. GI is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component (white rice, University of Sydney GI Database). Overall confidence is medium due to the HPB fat anomaly, the corrected fat value, and the composite nature of the dish.
notes: ZERO MACRO JUSTIFICATION (g4): The HPB Foodo source (G00010208) reports fat_g as 0.0 g/100g. This value is anomalous — grilled freshwater eel and half boiled egg both contain significant fat. The HPB value likely reflects only the protein/sauce component of the dish. Per the g4 zero-macro justification rule, fat_g has been corrected to 5.2 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy analogues: cooked eel (FDC 175136, ~14.9 g fat/100g) and hard-boiled egg (FDC 173424, ~10.6 g fat/100g) weighted at approximately 30% eel and 15% egg in the dish component, yielding an estimated 5.2 g fat/100g. Saturated_fat_g is set to 1.1 g/100g consistent with this corrected fat estimate. The HPB-reported 0.0 g fat is preserved in the nutrient_source notes for transparency. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (g3): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid breakdown for this specific composite Japanese rice bowl dish is not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a matched composite entry as of 2026-04-17. Sodium (280 mg/100g) and potassium (200 mg/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy analogues for cooked eel and egg components. Sugar (1.2 g/100g) and dietary fibre (0.2 g/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy analogues. GI NOTE: GI of 72 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate source (steamed white rice) from the University of Sydney GI Database. The HPB-recorded carbohydrate of 2.36 g/100g appears to reflect the non-rice component; the full bowl with rice will have substantially higher carbohydrate and glycaemic load. ONTOLOGY NOTE: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03315671 is used as an approximate parent-class anchor for donburi rice bowl dishes; no exact FoodOn entry exists for this specific dish. FoodEx2 code A0CKP (eel dish) is used as the primary ingredient anchor.
